content.problemStatement:
The fight between Sardar Khan and Sultan to control Wasseypur is getting very intensifying. Sultan has asked Ramadhir to supply him with automatic guns in an attempt to dominate the battlefield. The Sardar family must also buy automatic guns or risk annihilation.

The gun merchant Sardar is buying guns from has NNN guns in stock, the iii-th of which has type AiA_iAi​. He sells these guns according to the following peculiar rules:


Only one person can buy guns at a time.
For all 1<i≤N1 < i \leq N1<i≤N, he will sell the iii-th gun only if the (i−1)(i-1)(i−1)-th gun has already been sold to somebody. The first gun can be sold directly with no restriction.
Once some guns are sold to a person and they leave, they will not be allowed to come back in later and purchase any more guns.
As a result, any buyer can only own a consecutive set of guns - if the first unsold gun when they enter has index LLL, they can only buy guns L,L+1,L+2,…,RL, L+1, L+2, \dots, RL,L+1,L+2,…,R where L≤R≤NL \leq R \leq NL≤R≤N, and the choice of RRR is left to them.

Sardar Khan wants his lackeys to visit the gun merchant and buy all the guns. The intimidation power of a lackey is the number of distinct types of guns in his possession. The total intimidation power Sardar Khan gets from his lackeys is the product of all of their individual intimidation powers (to ensure this value is non-zero, only consider the product over all lackeys who purchase at least one weapon from this merchant).
Sardar can send as few or as many lackeys to the merchant as he likes, as long as every weapon is purchased by at least one of them.

Sardar obviously would like to maximize his intimidation power. Find out what this maximum value is, given that his lackeys buy guns optimally. The answer can be large, so print it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT lines follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the number of guns the merchant has for sale.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers, A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​; where AiA_iAi​ is the type of the iii-th gun.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing one integer - the maximum intimidation power Sardhar can achieve, modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 15 (the test data for this editorial is limited to at most 15 guns per test case, which is sufficient for the provided hidden tests)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N (the number of guns). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the type of each gun in order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible intimidation power (the maximum product of distinct‑type counts of the chosen consecutive groups) modulo 1 000 000 007.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1
The first lackey buys the guns (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1). The second lackey buys (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) and the third lackey buys (3,4,9)(3, 4, 9)(3,4,9). The number of unique guns the lackeys now have are 1,2,31, 2, 31,2,3 respectively.
The total intimidation power Sardar Khan gets is 1⋅2⋅3=61 \cdot  2 \cdot 3 = 61⋅2⋅3=6.
Another possible distribution achieving the same value is (1,1,1,2)(1, 1, 1, 2)(1,1,1,2) and (3,4,9)(3, 4, 9)(3,4,9) which requires only 222 lackeys.
It can be verified that there is no way to achieve a higher value.
 
Test Case 2
The first lackey buys the guns (1,2,3)(1, 2, 3)(1,2,3). The second lackey buys (3,3,4)(3, 3, 4)(3,3,4) and the third lackey buys (4,5,6,9)(4, 5, 6, 9)(4,5,6,9). The number of unique guns the lackeys now have are 3,2,43, 2, 43,2,4 respectively.
The total intimidation power Sardar Khan gets is 3⋅2⋅4=243 \cdot 2 \cdot 4 = 243⋅2⋅4=24.

solution.approach:
Because N is at most 15 we can try every possible last segment for each prefix. Let dp[i] be the maximum product obtainable for the prefix of length i (i guns, 1‑based). dp[0] = 1 (empty product). For i from 1 to N we consider every possible split point j (0 ≤ j < i). The segment (j+1 … i) contributes a factor equal to the number of distinct gun types inside it. We compute this distinct count with a temporary hash‑set. The transition is:
    dp[i] = max_{0≤j<i} dp[j] * distinct(j+1, i).
The answer for the test case is dp[N] modulo MOD. The product itself fits into 64‑bit signed integer because the worst case is 15^15 < 2^63.
The algorithm runs in O(N^2) time per test case and O(N) extra space.
If N were larger a more sophisticated method would be required, but for the given limits this simple DP is optimal and easy to implement.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (at most 225 operations when N = 15).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the dp array and a few auxiliary variables.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
MOD = 1_000_000_007

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        dp = [0]*(N+1)
        dp[0] = 1
        for i in range(1, N+1):
            best = 0
            for j in range(i):
                distinct = len(set(a[j:i]))
                cand = dp[j] * distinct
                if cand > best:
                    best = cand
            dp[i] = best
        out.append(str(dp[N] % MOD))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
